title-group.article-title: Exercise training ameliorates myocardial dysfunction through fibronectin mediated mechanotransduction in a swine model of ischemic heart disease

abstract.p: Exercise training has been shown to reverse cardiac dysfunction in patients and animal models of coronary artery disease; however, the underlying mechanisms have not been fully elucidated. Transmembrane integrins that connect the extracellular matrix (ECM) and intracellular cytoskeleton are important for mechanotransduction in cardiomyocytes. We tested the hypothesis that exercise training would increase cardiac contractile function by modulating the adhesion force between integrins and ECM proteins and subsequent cell signaling and stiffness in myocytes from ischemic porcine hearts. Ameroid occluders were surgically placed around the proximal left circumflex coronary artery of adult Yucatan pigs. Animals subsequently completed either a sedentary or endurance exercise (treadmill run 5 days/week for 14 weeks) protocol, after which myocardium was isolated from nonoccluded and collateral-dependent regions. The collateral-dependent myocardial region exhibited increased fibrosis, inflammatory cytokines, and collagen I and III levels, which were ameliorated with exercise-training. Exercise also increased fibronectin and β1 integrin, and decreased β3 integrin levels in collateral-dependent myocardium compared to that of sedentary pigs. Atomic force microscopy revealed that an increase in fibronectin-integrin adhesion force was mediated by α5β1 and αvβ3 integrins in cardiac myocytes of exercise-trained pigs. Exercise training increased mechanical stiffness in cardiomyocytes compared to that in sedentary swine. Fibronectin- and exercise-induced force generation in trabeculae from collateral-dependent myocardium were each decreased by focal adhesion kinase (FAK) inhibition. These data demonstrate that exercise training increases force generation in cardiomyocytes by attenuating inflammation and by promoting fibronectin mediated FAK activation, suggesting potential targeting of this mechanotransduction pathway for therapeutic development.

abstract.title: New & Noteworthy
abstract.p: Exercise produces cardioprotective effects and reverses cardiac dysfunction, but underlying cellular and molecular mechanisms are not fully identified. This study revealed that endurance exercise increased fibronectin expression in myocardium of ischemic swine hearts and enhanced myocyte adhesion with α5β1 integrin, cell stiffness, and force generation, which was blunted by focal adhesion kinase inhibition. Thus, endurance exercise reverses cardiac dysfunction by promoting fibronectin interactions with integrins supporting this mechanotransduction pathway as a potential therapeutic target.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Ischemic heart disease is the leading form of cardiovascular disease in the US [1] and is one of the most common risk factors for development of systolic or diastolic dysfunction that results in heart failure [1]. Ischemic heart disease also is associated with progressive impairment of cardiac function resulting from cardiac remodeling [2]. Exercise training improves heart function in humans and animal models of coronary artery disease and prevents cardiac remodeling. Numerous studies have shown impaired myocardial contraction and left ventricular ejection fraction, as well as myocardial ischemia using the well-established and clinically relevant porcine model of chronic coronary artery occlusion [3–6]. We have previously reported that exercise training improves cardiac function by enhancing calcium (Ca2+) sensitization of cardiac myofilament molecules in coronary artery occluded porcine hearts [7]. In addition, we have also revealed that exercise training enhanced both endothelial and smooth muscle dependent vasoreactivity in collateral-dependent coronary arteries [8–10]. However, the cellular and molecular mechanisms by which exercise training improves cardiac function in chronic coronary artery occlusion are not yet completely understood.

Preservation of cardiac architecture is critical for proper contractile force transmission that relies on an intricate network of extracellular matrix (ECM) proteins. The myocardial ECM contains various types of proteins and signaling molecules that maintain the dynamic balance and integrity of cardiac structure. Collagen I forms thick fibers and confers tensile strength, while collagen III forms thin fibers and maintains elasticity [11, 12]. In addition, glycosaminoglycans, glycoproteins, and proteoglycans are components of ECM, where large amounts of proteases and growth factors are bound [11]. Cardiomyocytes and interstitial cells such as fibroblasts, vascular endothelial and smooth muscle cells, and immune cells are enmeshed into the collagen-based matrix and continuously interact with the ECM, thus sensing alterations in the microenvironment, including the ischemic condition [13]. Cardiac fibroblasts are the most abundant interstitial cells in the adult mammalian myocardium and regulate the matrix network [13]. In adult hearts, cardiac fibroblasts remain quiescent, exhibiting minimal proliferative activity. However, the loss of cardiomyocytes due to ischemic heart disease or myocardial infarction leads to an expansion of collagen content and an increase in the interstitial space [14–16]. The composition of ECM dynamically changes during coronary artery disease [17]. Within ECM families, fibronectin promotes mechanisms of cardiac repair that stabilize cardiac function after cardiac injury [18]. Fibronectin promotes the adhesion of cardiomyocytes and is highly expressed in early development, remains stable in adult tissue, and demonstrates increased expression in the hypertrophic and injured myocardium. We have previously reported that fibronectin induces force generation in mouse papillary muscles via α5β1 integrin-protein kinase A activation and fibronectin-integrin adhesion force is greater in contracted myocytes than in cells under relaxed conditions [19, 20]. Exercise training has been shown to prevent fibrosis in various cardiac diseases and thus, improve cardiac function [21–24]. Furthermore, previous studies have reported that exercise training reverses the severity of cardiac fibrosis and is coupled with enhanced cardiac function in post coronary artery ligation in a murine model [25, 26]. Hence, we hypothesized that exercise training would increase fibronectin-integrin adhesion and modulate downstream signaling to enhance cardiac function in a porcine model of ischemic heart disease.

To test this hypothesis, we used the well-established swine model of chronic coronary artery occlusion and exercise training to determine mechanotransduction characteristics, cell adhesion force, and cell stiffness in isolated cardiac myocytes using atomic force microscopy (AFM), as well as measure force generation in trabeculae muscle fibers. We also assessed ECM, integrins and cytokines expression in porcine myocardium isolated from nonoccluded and collateral-dependent regions. Our data demonstrate that exercise training improves cardiac remodeling by decreasing fibrosis and inflammatory cytokines and increases the adhesion force and cell stiffness of cardiac myocytes, consequently improving cardiac force generation through fibronectin mediated FAK activation.

sec.title: Materials.
sec.p: Anti-collagen I (ab34710), anti-CD11b (ab133357) and anti-α5 integrin (ab112183) antibodies were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA, USA). Anti-fibronectin (SAB4500974), anti-collagen III (SAB4500367), laminin (L9393) and anti-β3 Integrin (AB2984) antibodies were purchased from Millipore Sigma (Burlington, MA, USA). Anti-β1 Integrin (MAB17783) was purchased from R&D Systems and GAPDH, (926–32211) was purchased from Advanced Immunochemical. Anti-αv integrin (PA5–27272), Alexa Fluor® secondary antibodies and Prolong Gold Antifade mounting medium with DAPI were purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Vectashield® mounting media was purchased from Vector laboratories (Burlingame, CA, USA). IRDye® secondary antibodies were purchased from LI-COR Biosciences (Lincoln, Nebraska, USA). All other chemicals and reagents were from Millipore Sigma (Burlington, MA, USA).

sec.title: Experimental animals and surgical instrumentation.
sec.p: All animal protocols were approved by the Texas A&M University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee in accordance with the American Association of Accreditation of Laboratory Animal Care Procedures. Furthermore, all methods conformed to the National Institutes of Health (NIH) Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. All experiments were done in a blinded study manner.

Adult female Yucatan miniature swine (age: 6–7 months, body weight: 14–24 kg, Sinclair Research Center, Auxvasse, MO) were surgically instrumented with an ameroid occluder around the proximal left circumflex coronary artery as previously described [7, 10]. Animals were pre-anesthetized with glycopyrrolate (0.004 mg/kg; i.m.), midazolam (0.5 mg/kg; i.m.) and ketamine (20 mg/kg; i.m.). Animals were masked and anesthesia was induced with 3% isoflurane. Pigs were then intubated, and anesthesia was maintained with 2–3% isoflurane and supplemental O2 throughout aseptic surgery. A left lateral thoracotomy was performed in the fourth intercostal space and the underlying pericardium was opened to expose the left circumflex artery. The proximal left circumflex artery was dissected free of surrounding tissue and an ameroid constrictor (2.5- to 3.5-mm inner diameter) was placed around the artery. The diameter of the occluder was selected upon visual inspection of the artery to initially provide a secure but nonconstrictive fit. The pericardium was then closed, and the thoracotomy was repaired in tissue layers. Antibiotic Excede (ceftiofur 5 mg/kg, i.m.) was administered immediately prior to surgery. During surgery, animals received the following drugs as necessary: pancuronium (0.1 mg/kg; i.v.; neuromuscular blocker), atropine (0.5 mg/kg; antimuscarinic), epinephrine (10 μg/kg; adrenergic agent), and lidocaine (1 mg/kg, i.v.; anti-arrhythmic). Bupivacaine (local anesthetic) was applied topically to the third, fourth, and fifth intercostal spaces (2 ml per space) prior to cutting and on muscle tissue along the suture line (2 ml) during closure. Immediately prior to surgery and during surgical recovery, animals received buprenorphine hydrochloride (0.02 mg/kg, i.v.; analgesic) every 3–4 hr, as needed for pain relief. Immediately following surgery, pigs also received ketofen (3.0 mg/kg, i.v.; non-steroidal anti-inflammatory).

sec.title: Sedentary and Exercise Protocols.
sec.p: Our group has historically used only female swine for exercise studies as we had concerns regarding potential aggression in male swine during treadmill running and thereby, safety concerns for our trainers. The data generated for this manuscript were collected at a time that we were using only female swine. Eight weeks post-operatively, pigs were randomly assigned to sedentary (n=10) and exercise-trained (n=8) groups. Exercise-trained pigs underwent a progressive treadmill exercise training protocol (5 days/week for 14 weeks) as previously described [9, 10, 27–30]. Efficacy of the exercise training regimen was determined by comparing heart-to-body weight ratio and skeletal muscle citrate synthase enzyme activity as previously described [7–9]. It was confirmed at termination that all animals used in the current study displayed 100% occlusion of the left circumflex artery at the site of the ameroid.

sec.title: Preparation of porcine cardiomyocyte isolation.
sec.p: Following completion of the 14-week exercise-training or sedentary regimen, animals were anesthetized using xylazine (2.25 mg/kg, i.m.) and ketamine (35 mg/kg, i.m.). Pigs were then intubated and ventilated with 3% isoflurane with supplemental O2. A left lateral thoracotomy was performed in the fourth intercostal space, followed by heparin (500 U/kg, i.v.) administration through an ear catheter. The heart was removed, placed in iced Krebs bicarbonate buffer (0–4°C), and weighed. Porcine cardiomyocytes were prepared as previously described [7, 31]. Briefly, sections of the left ventricular wall (1 cm3) were removed from both the nonoccluded (left anterior descending artery supplied) and collateral-dependent (formerly left circumflex supplied) regions and placed into cold (4°C) relaxing solution (2 mM EGTA, 5 mM MgCl2, 4 mM ATP, 10 mM imidazole, and 100 mM KCl; pH 7.0). The myocardial regions were cut into smaller pieces and gently crushed with a cold mortar and pestle. Cells were released by gentle trituration with a fire-polished glass pipette, filtered through a 200-μm pore-sized sterile mesh, and resuspended in the cold relaxing solution for further use. Cells were allowed to settle to the bottom of a 30-mm culture dish and used for AFM. For experiments with integrin-blocking monoclonal antibodies (mAb), the myocyte preparations were incubated for 30 min with α5 (HMα5–1, 60nM) (BD Bioscience, San Jose, CA), αv (CD51, 60nM) (ThermoFisher) or α11 (MAB4235, 60nM) (R&D systems) mAb prior to the AFM measurements.

sec.title: AFM measurements of adhesion force and stiffness.
sec.p: The force contact mode of AFM operation was employed for measurements of adhesion force. The adhesion forces were detected and quantified from the retraction curve of the AFM probe. The measurement of relative cortical membrane stiffness (elastic modulus) was derived from the approach curve as previously described [19, 20]. Briefly, AFM experiments were performed using a Bioscope system (model 3A, Digital Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA, USA; currently, Bruker AFM Probes Americas), which was coupled with Axiovert 100 TV inverted microscope (Carl Zeiss). The Bioscope system was equipped with Nanoscope IIIa controller and Nanoscope III sortware (Version 5.12). The AFM probes were silicon nitride microlevers with conical tips (model MLCT-AUHW). Tip diameters were < 40 nm, and the mean spring constant was ~14.4 ± 0.6 pN/nm. For each experiment, the position of the probe was controlled to repeatedly touch and retract (z-axis) from the cell surface. Force curves were recorded for these repeated cycles of probe approach and retraction at 0.5-Hz scan frequency and a z-axis movement of 800 nm. In each set of experiments, we sampled 500 force curves taken from 10 randomly chosen cells (derived from 5 hearts, with 50 curves per cell) for each treatment. The AFM probe tip was selectively positioned between the nucleus and the edge of the cell. The adhesion force measurements obtained from all retraction curves were analyzed as force-density relationships. Smooth plots of relative density of adhesion force were obtained with standard kernel density estimation method. The cell cortical stiffness (i.e., elastic modulus or cell resistance to shape deformation) was calculated from the force curves based on tip displacement and membrane indentation, as previously detailed [19, 20].

sec.title: Force measurements in trabeculae fibers.
sec.p: Trabeculae fiber from collateral-dependent myocardium were isolated from sedentary and exercise-trained pigs. Subsequently, the trabeculae fiber was mounted within a muscle measurement suite (Scientific Instruments, Germany) and superfused at the rate of 1.5 ml/min continuously with Tyrode solution (in mM: 136.9 NaCl, 5.0 KCl, 1.8 CaCl2, 1.5 MgCl2, 0.4 NaH2PO4, 11.9 NaHCO3, 5.0 D-glucose, pH 7.4). The frequency of stimulation was increased stepwise, i.e., 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, and 1 (3ms pulse duration), at 34 °C to determine the force-frequency relationship and fibers manifesting a positive force-frequency relationship over frequencies of stimulation (0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 Hz) were selected for further experimentation, as we have previously described [7]. Then the experimental protocols were done at 1.0 Hz that consisted of: (1) fibers were superfused with Tyrode solution containing either 35nM fibronectin (FN) (440 kD, Invitrogen Corporation, Grand Island, NY) or 35nM bovine serum albumin (BSA. Amersham Life Science, Arlington Heights, IL) for 15 min before starting to record data; and (2) for FAK inhibitor studies, the fibers were incubated with the cell-permeable FAK inhibitor PF573228 (FAK-I, 1.0μM. MedChemExpress, Junction, NJ) for 15 min before incubating with FN.

sec.title: Masson’s Trichrome staining
sec.p: Left ventricular myocardium from both the nonoccluded (left anterior descending artery supplied) and collateral-dependent (formerly left circumflex supplied) regions were cut and immediately frozen it in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80°C or placed in plastic molds, covered in Tissue Tek (OCT, Sakura, Torrence, CA, USA), then frozen and stored at −80°C until use. Myocardial tissues were then sectioned (10 μm) using a cryostat (−20 °C). Sections were air-dried for 30 min and then fixed overnight in Bouin’s solution at room temperature. Masson’s trichrome staining was used to detect fibrosis as described earlier [32]. Cross-sections were stained in Weigert’s hematoxylin for 15 min, washed in distilled water, and then tap water for 5 min. Cardiac cross-sections were then stained with Beibrich scarlet-acid fuchsin for 15 min, then washed in dH2O for 5 min. After differentiation in 2.5% phosphomolybdic-phosphotungstic acid solution for 15 min, samples were then transferred into 2.5% aniline blue solution for 12 min. Cardiac sections were differentiated in 1% acetic acid solution for 3 min, dehydrated in 95% and 100% ethanol, then cleared in xylene. Cardiac myocytes stain a bright red and collagen fibers bold blue. Sections were captured on a Zeiss Axiovision microscope and software and quantified using the NIH Image J analysis program. 3 field of view (FOV) per heart were used in the analyses of calculating the area of fibrotic region and the percentage of fibrotic region (% of connective tissue) per total area of the field was presented in the quantitative analyses.

sec.title: Immunofluorescent staining
sec.p: Cardiac tissue sections were fixed in cooled acetone for 60 min at −20 °C. Samples were then blocked with 10% serum from the host of the secondary antibody in TBS (100 μl) for 30 min. The appropriate primary antibodies (collagen I, collagen III, fibronectin, laminin and CD11b) were applied in blocking solution (1:100 dilution in phosphate buffered saline, PBS), and sections were incubated for 1 hour at room temperature. After three washes with PBS, the secondary antibodies Alexa Fluor 488 or Alexa Fluor 594 were placed on the section (1:200 dilution in PBS) and incubated for 30 min. The section was then mounted using ProLong mounting media with DAPI (4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole). Images were acquired using a Zeiss microscope at 10x, 40x, or 63x. Fluorescence signal intensities were quantified using the NIH Image J analysis program [33, 34]. We used 15 fields of view (FOV) per heart in the quantitative analyses. The fluorescence signal intensities of collagen I, collagen III, fibronectin, and laminin were expressed as percentages relative to the sedentary non-occluded group. CD11-positive cells were counted and expressed as a percentage of the total number of nuclei.

sec.title: Western immunoblot analysis.
sec.p: Protein expression was quantified by Western immunoblot analysis, as described in previous studies [7, 35]. Briefly, protein lysate was prepared from left ventricular myocardial segments isolated from nonoccluded and collateral-dependent regions of sedentary and exercise-trained pigs. Protein lysates (10μg) were separated using a 4–20 % gradient SDS-polyacrylamide gel and transferred to a nitrocellulose membrane, and Western blot analysis was performed. The following dilutions of the primary antibodies were used: β1 Integrin (1:1000), β3 Integrin (1:1000), α5 integrin (1:500), αv integrin, (1:500) and GAPDH (1:1000). After a Tris-buffered saline wash in triplicate, membranes were incubated with the appropriate horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies or LI-COR secondary antibodies. Protein detection was conducted with a sensitive enhanced chemiluminescence or LI-COR system and visualized via an image processor (Fuji LAS-400 Mini) or LI-COR image processor. GAPDH protein was used as the loading control. Densitometry analyses on the resulting bands were determined with NIH Image J [33], normalized to GAPDH, and expressed as fold change relative to sedentary non-occluded group.

sec.title: Cytokine analysis in porcine myocardium
sec.p: Total RNA was extracted using RNeasy kit (Qiagen, Valencia, CA), according to the manufacturer’s instructions as described in our previous studies [36, 37]. The quality and quantity of isolated RNA was determined using a NanoDrop spectrophotometer (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA). RNA was converted to cDNA using the Superscript III cDNA synthesis kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Primers specific to interleukin 6 (IL6, Ss03384604_u1), interleukin 1β (IL1β, Ss03393804_m1), and monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP1, Ss03394377_m1) from Thermo Fisher Scientific were used for real-time PCR. Ribosomal protein L19 (RPL19) amplification was used as the housekeeping gene for all samples, and the difference in the relative expression of cytokines between groups was calculated using the 2−ΔΔCt method [34, 37] and presented as fold change relative to the sedentary group.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Skeletal muscle citrate synthase, body weight, and heart-to-body weight ratio were compared between animal treatment groups using Student’s t-tests. Adhesion force and stiffness by AFM were analyzed by independent (unpaired) student’s t test. Left circumflex coronary artery occlusion and exercise training effects on fibrosis, ECM, integrin, and CD11b+ cells were analyzed using two-way ANOVA (occlusion x exercise) to assess interaction or main effect. When a main or interaction effect was found, Tukey’s HSD post hoc test was used for multiple comparisons to determine between-group (i.e., occlusion) or within-group (i.e., exercise training) differences. Levene’s statistic was performed to test the homogeneity of variances. Force measurements data with exogenous soluble fibronectin or focal adhesion kinase inhibitor were analyzed using one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s HSD post hoc test using SPSS software (IBM Corp, Armonk, New York, USA). For western blot data, Brown-Forsythe ANOVA to test for unequal variances among groups, followed by Welch’s ANOVA was performed. All values are expressed as mean ± SEM; p ≤ 0.05 was considered significant. All graphs were generated with Prism 8 and 10 (GraphPad Software, La Jolla, CA).

sec.title: Effectiveness of the exercise training program.
sec.p: The effectiveness of the 14-wk exercise-training regimen was determined by comparing skeletal muscle oxidative enzyme activity and heart weight-to-body weight ratio in exercise-trained and sedentary pigs (Table 1) using Student’s t-test. Citrate synthase activity increased significantly in triceps medial head and deltoid muscles in exercise-trained compared with sedentary pigs (Table 1). Although body weight did not differ statistically between exercise-trained and sedentary pigs, heart weight-to-body weight ratio was significantly greater in exercise-trained compared with sedentary animals at the end of the study, providing evidence of exercise training-induced cardiac hypertrophy.

sec.title: Mechanisms by which exercise training ameliorates fibrotic remodeling.
sec.p: To determine the effect of exercise training on the remodeling of chronically occluded hearts, we quantified the degree of fibrosis in the myocardial tissue of sedentary and exercise-trained pigs. As shown by representative images in Figure 1A, Masson’s trichrome staining was carried out on both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent left ventricular myocardium from sedentary and exercise-trained swine. Images were obtained with x10 (numerical aperture, NA = 0.5) objectives. Data in Figure 1B revealed that coronary artery occlusion caused an increase in the percent of connective tissue in the collateral-dependent region of the myocardium compared to that in the nonoccluded region of both sedentary and exercise-trained groups. Importantly, exercise training significantly reduced the fibrosis in the collateral-dependent region compared to that of the sedentary group (Figure 1B; 3.56 ± 0.30 vs. 2.48 ± 0.16%, 30%; p<0.05, n = 8).

Next, we determined the expression of key ECM proteins, collagen I, collagen III, fibronectin, and laminin to examine how coronary artery occlusion and/or endurance exercise training contributes to cardiac remodeling. Representative images of collagen I and collagen III from both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent left ventricular myocardial regions from sedentary and exercise training groups are shown in Figure 2A. As shown in Figure 2B, coronary artery occlusion increased both collagen I (top panel) and collagen III (bottom panel) in the collateral-dependent compared with the nonoccluded region of sedentary pigs (+157.1% and +235.7 %, respectively, p < 0.0001). Exercise training reduced both collagen I and collagen III expression in the collateral-dependent, but not the nonoccluded, region compared to that of sedentary pigs (−93.6 % and −196.3%, respectively, p < 0.0001) (Figure 2B). Representative images of fibronectin from both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent myocardial regions from sedentary and exercise trained group are shown in Figure 2C. While chronic occlusion caused no significant changes in fibronectin expression (+24.5 % increase in collateral-dependent region vs. the nonoccluded region of sedentary group, p=0.29), exercise training significantly increased fibronectin expression in the collateral-dependent (+121.0% vs. sedentary collateral-dependent, p < 0.0001), but not the nonoccluded region (Figure 2D). Lastly, representative images of laminin from both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent myocardial regions of sedentary and exercise-trained pigs are shown in Figure 2E. Our data reveal that myocardial laminin expression was not altered by chronic occlusion nor exercise training (Figure 2F). These data demonstrate that exercise-training modulates ECM proteins and consequently decreases fibrosis in the chronically occluded hearts.

sec.title: Exercise-training increases β1 integrin and decreases β3 integrin proteins with no changes in α5 and αv integrins.
sec.p: Since exercise-training increased fibronectin that binds to α5β1 and αvβ3 integrins, we then assessed α5, αv, β1 and β3 integrin protein levels in nonoccluded and collateral-dependent myocardium of both sedentary and exercise-trained pigs. Representative immunoblots for β1 and β3 integrins and the loading control, GAPDH, are shown in Figure 3A. Exercise training significantly increased β1 integrin protein content in both nonoccluded (+1.0 fold; p < 0.0005) and collateral-dependent (+2.2 fold; p < 0.0001) regions compared with that from sedentary pigs (Figure 3B). In contrast, β3 integrin protein levels were significantly decreased by exercise training in both nonoccluded (−0.3 fold; p < 0.05) and collateral-dependent myocardium (−0.7; p < 0.0001) when compared to sedentary pigs (Figures 3C). Interestingly, in the collateral-dependent region, β3 integrin was significantly increased in myocardium from sedentary animals and significantly decreased in exercise-trained pigs compared with the nonoccluded region. As shown in Figure 3D, there was a 2-fold increase in α5 integrin in the collateral-dependent region of exercise-trained swine when compared to other groups, but this increase did not attain statistical significance (p<0.165). Additionally, there is a tendency for increased αv protein levels in collateral-dependent myocardium from both sedentary and exercise-trained pigs, but the values did not achieve statistical significance (Figure 3E). Thus, these data demonstrate that while exercise-training increased β1 integrin and decreased β3 integrin, it did not affect either α5 or αv integrin protein levels in the occluded hearts.

sec.title: Exercise-training increases adhesion force associated with fibronectin-β1 integrins and myocardial cell stiffness.
sec.p: To evaluate the stiffness of the cardiomyocyte membrane and the adhesion force between fibronectin and the cardiomyocyte surface, AFM probes coated with fibronectin were used on non-contracting cardiomyocytes. Since cardiomyocyte preparations from the collateral-dependent myocardial region of sedentary animals did not consistently provide high quality cells, cardiomyocytes from nonoccluded regions of both sedentary and exercise-trained pigs were evaluated in the AFM studies. The number of adhesion events that were observed are plotted as a histogram and fitted with Gaussian distribution to resolve fibronectin-integrin bond adhesion force (Figure 4A). The probability of adhesion was significantly increased (54% vs. 72%, p < 0.05) in cardiomyocytes of exercise-trained compared with sedentary pigs (Figure 4A). The adhesion force of fibronectin-integrin was significantly increased (33%, p < 0.05) in myocytes from exercise-trained compared with sedentary animals (Figure 4B). To further delineate the specificity of the fibronectin-integrin interaction, cardiomyocytes were pre-treated with integrin monoclonal antibodies. As shown in Figure 4B, both α5- and αv-integrin antibodies significantly reduced the adhesion force in myocytes from sedentary and exercise-trained groups. In myocytes from sedentary pigs, α5 and αv antibodies inhibited by 25% and 66%, respectively. In myocytes from exercise-trained animals, α5 and αv antibodies inhibited the binding by 75% and 30%, respectively. The control α11 integrin antibody treatment in both sedentary and exercise-trained groups did not significantly change the adhesion force (Figure 4B). We further computed cortical membrane stiffness from our force curve data as described in the Methods section. Exercise training significantly increased cardiomyocytes stiffness by 130%, when compared to myocytes from the sedentary animals (Figure 4C). Altogether, the increase in adhesion force and cell stiffness in the myocytes from exercise-trained animals indicate that exercise-training enhances the cell-ECM interactions through fibronectin-β1 integrin, and promotes greater force generation in the myocytes.

sec.title: Exercise training-induced or fibronectin-induced force generation in porcine trabeculae fibers is mediated by FAK.
sec.p: We have previously reported that exercise training enhanced force generation in both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent myocardial regions in our porcine model [7] and that fibronectin increased the force production in mouse papillary fibers [19, 20]. Since we observed in this study that fibronectin is increased in the collateral-dependent myocardium region of the exercise-trained pigs, we speculated that exercise training-induced force generation in porcine trabeculae muscle fiber is due to increased fibronectin-integrin mediated FAK activation. Figure 5 reveals that exercise training increased force generation of cardiac trabeculae from the collateral-dependent myocardium compared to trabeculae from the same region of sedentary pigs (30%p < 0.02). Fibronectin treatment significantly increased force generation in the trabeculae from the collateral-dependent myocardium of sedentary pigs (28%p < 0.02), but not of exercise trained animals (p = 0.93). Force generation in the presence of FAK inhibition (Figure 5) is significantly decreased from both control and fibronectin-stimulated conditions in trabeculae from collateral-dependent myocardium of both sedentary (22%; p < 0.0001) and exercise-trained pigs (36%; p < 0.01).

sec.title: Exercise training decreases inflammatory mediators in the collateral-dependent region of porcine myocardium.
sec.p: Since inflammatory status is a critical modulator that mediates ECM and reparative responses following stress from coronary artery disease [38], we investigated inflammatory mediators in collateral-dependent myocardium. Since several studies have shown CD11b, IL1β, IL6, and MCP1 are consistently increased in animal models of myocardial infarction and targeted molecules for potential therapy, [39–44] we investigated to see whether exercise-training would decrease these inflammatory markers in the occluded hearts. Representative fluorescent images of CD11b (green), DAPI (blue), and merged images from both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent myocardial regions of sedentary and exercise-trained groups are shown in Figure 6A. While coronary artery occlusion significantly increased CD11b+ cells in the myocardium of sedentary pigs (+13.1-fold vs. sedentary nonoccluded region, p < 0.0001), exercise-training significantly blunted CD11b+ cell infiltration in the collateral-dependent region (−9.1-fold vs. sedentary collateral-dependent region, p < 0.001) (Figure 6A). We also found that exercise-training significantly decreased expression of pro-inflammatory genes, IL6, IL1β, and MCP1 in collateral-dependent myocardial regions, but only MCP1 in the nonoccluded myocardial region (Figures 6B and C), indicating that exercise-training significantly decreases inflammatory status of the chronically occluded myocardium.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The present study investigates cellular and molecular adaptations of cardiac function with exercise training in a porcine model of chronic coronary occlusion, an animal model that is highly clinically relevant to human cardiovascular pathophysiology. Our results demonstrate that exercise training provides significant protection against collagen I and III expression and thus, fibrosis in hearts with coronary artery disease. Fibronectin augments force generation, suggesting the increase in fibronectin protein levels with exercise training is indeed cardioprotective in the setting of coronary artery disease. Our data show that the underlying molecular mechanisms that enhance cardiac function after exercise training appear to be driven in part by increased mechanotransduction signaling via fibronectin-integrin-FAK axis (Figure 7). Hence, our study provides the first documentation of ECM alterations by exercise training that enhances fibronectin and mechanotransduction as a potential therapeutic target.

In the present study, all pigs were instrumented with an ameroid occluder around the proximal left circumflex coronary artery, with myocardium supplied by the corresponding left anterior descending artery serving as the within-animal control. It is important to note that all animals were subject to chronic occlusion; therefore, our study does not aim to compare whether heart weight increased solely due to occlusion. However, previous studies in the swine model suggests that chronic occlusion alone does not lead to changes in heart/body weight ratio [45] [10]. This result supports that the heart-to-body weight ratio observed in sedentary and exercise-trained swine with occlusion closely aligns with values reported in previous non-occluded pigs [10]. Moreover, at the conclusion of our study, we verified complete occlusion of the left circumflex artery at the ameroid occluder site in all animals; the extent of ischemia or injury among the animals was not evaluated.

In utilizing a pig model with an ameroid constrictor to induce chronic coronary artery stenosis, it is observed that the resultant heart damage and pathophysiological alterations significantly depend on the occlusion’s specifics, such as its location, timing, and degree of severity. This method results in a gradual stenosis progression, mirroring the human condition by allowing collateral vessels to form, offering a realistic simulation of human cardiovascular conditions [46, 47]. Histological examinations have further underscored the model’s validity for studying stress-induced ischemic heart disease rather than serving as a model for myocardial infarction. Specifically, it reveals a modest infarct size, averaging ~1% of the left ventricular myocardium, showcasing the model’s ability to closely replicate the clinical scenarios observed in human ischemic patients [3, 48].

Chronic coronary artery diseases contribute to cardiac fibrotic remodeling, including in porcine models [49–52]. Increased cardiac fibrotic tissues contribute to pathological ECM remodeling, leading to abnormalities in matrix composition and quality that induce cardiac dysfunction [15, 17]. Initially, ECM deposition serves as a protective mechanism that supports tissue regeneration [2, 9, 53]. However, when ECM deposition, especially of collagen, becomes excessive, it leads to an impairment of cardiac contractility. Collagen types I and III are central to the structure of the cardiac ECM, playing pivotal roles in preserving the functional integrity of the heart. In a healthy heart, these collagen types provide essential structural support to muscle cells, which is critical for proper cardiac function. Collagen type I, known for its stiff fibrillar structure, contributes tensile strength, while collagen type III enhances the elasticity of the network, aiding in the storage of kinetic energy. The distinct properties of these collagen types are vital for the normal operation and structural integrity of the heart [54–56]. In conditions like cardiomyopathy, there are marked changes in the expression levels of these collagens, leading to alterations in the cardiac ECM. Diseases such as dilated cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, ischemic cardiomyopathy, and hypertensive heart disease are associated with specific changes in the levels of collagen I and III. These alterations affect myocardial stiffness, thereby impacting both diastolic and systolic functions of the heart. This highlights the critical role of collagen types I and III not only in maintaining cardiac health but also in the pathological remodeling observed across various heart diseases. The changes in ECM deposition offer insights into the disease state and progression and have significant implications for the functional properties of heart tissue [11, 15, 56–58].

While collagen is the most studied ECM protein in the heart, cardiac ECM contains varied proteins that indicate different structural and functional adaptability [58]. For example, laminin and fibronectin are glycoproteins that play important roles in development and cardiac remodeling during pathology. Laminin is the first ECM glycoprotein seen in the development process and forms crosslinks to maintain fine molecular anchors [58]. Fibronectin contains a cell-binding domain with the RGD sequence, which is essential for cell interactions with integrins. Fibronectin is soluble in the blood but insoluble in the ECM. Fibronectin stabilizes other ECM components by forming crosslinks and regulates cell shape and movements. Corroborating previous findings, our data demonstrate that chronic occlusion/stenosis of the left circumflex coronary artery increases fibrosis (Figure 1) and exercise training decreases fibrosis by reducing collagen I and III and increasing fibronectin protein levels in collateral-dependent myocardium (Figure 2).

Exercise training produces a cardioprotective effect in various cardiovascular pathologies, including aging, hypertension, coronary artery disease, and cardiomyopathy [21, 23, 59]. We have previously reported a cardioprotective effect of exercise training in this porcine model of chronic coronary occlusion [7–9]. Exercise training enhances systolic wall thickening and transmural myocardial blood flow in areas supplied by the left circumflex coronary artery. This suggests an improvement in myocardial function and an increase in coronary flow reserve in the collateral-dependent myocardium in response to exercise training [3]. Additionally, we previously observed that exercise training increased cardiac myocyte contractility by increasing Ca2+ sensitization of cardiac myofilament proteins [7]. In the present study, we further tested mechanisms that underlie enhanced force generation, including adhesion probability between fibronectin and integrin, and cell stiffness (elasticity) using isolated myocytes from the sedentary and exercise-trained pigs with AFM (Figures 4 and 5). We found that exercise training enhanced adhesion force and cell stiffness in porcine cardiomyocytes (Figure 4), suggesting the FN-coated AFM probe engaged more strongly with α5β1 integrins, due to the increase in apparent cell stiffness. This finding reflected an increase in the force generation status of the contractile apparatus in the myocytes from exercise-trained animals. However, further studies are warranted to examine this phenomenon in the myocytes isolated from occluded regions of the myocardium. We further revealed that enhanced force generation is due to increased fibronectin and integrin-mediated focal adhesion complex in cardiac myocytes. Exogenous fibronectin does not increase force generation in myocytes from hearts of exercise-trained animals while myocytes from sedentary pigs displayed significantly elevated force generation by exogenously administrated fibronectin (Figure 5). Notably, exercise training significantly elevated fibronectin protein levels in collateral-dependent myocardial regions.

Among the integrin subunits, α3, α5, αv, β1, and β3 are primary integrins that interact with fibronectin [60], thus, we tested the protein content of these integrin subunits in porcine myocardium. Exercise training increased β1 integrin and decreased β3 integrin in myocardium of both nonoccluded and collateral-dependent regions (Figures 3A, B and C); though, we did not see significant changes in α5 and αv integrin levels in myocardium of either nonoccluded and collateral-dependent regions (Figures 3D and E). However, AFM data showed that α5 and αv antibodies had differential effects on adhesion force between sedentary and exercised groups (Figure 4B). Taken together, we propose that αvβ3 is predominately active/functional in myocytes from sedentary animals, whereas, in exercised-trained group, α5β1 integrin is active/functional. Our laboratory has reported that fibronectin increased force generation in cardiomyocytes and α5β1 integrin exhibited a strong affinity to fibronectin compared to other integrin subunits [19, 20]. The β1 integrin displays a protective role in chronic β-adrenergic receptor-induced heart failure [61]. In contrast, β3 integrin promotes cardiac fibroblasts inducing cardiac fibrosis while depletion of β3 decreases collagen accumulation [62]. Hence, the enhanced force generation in exercise-trained myocytes appears to be the result of elevated fibronectin and associated focal adhesion by augmented β1 integrin interaction.

β1 integrin is crucial in fibroblasts, playing a significant role in cardiac fibrosis and hypertrophy. As key contributors to ECM deposition, fibroblasts provide essential structural support and regulate cardiac function. Dysregulation of β1 integrin processes can lead to fibrosis, negatively impacting heart function. In cardiomyocytes, β1 integrin is vital for preserving cellular structure and function, especially in mechanotransduction that is essential for the heart’s adaptation to mechanical stress. β1 integrin’s interactions with the extracellular matrix and participation in signaling pathways are critical for cardiomyocyte functionality and the cardiac response to diverse stimuli [38, 63, 64]. We previously reported the role of α5β1 integrin in interacting with fibronectin within the ECM, rather than its interaction with fibroblasts directly [20]. Further studies are necessary to determine the expression of α- and β-integrins in the myocardial and fibroblast cells from sedentary vs. exercise-trained pigs and to explore their specific functional roles in cardiomyocytes and fibroblasts.

Several studies have demonstrated that exercise training enhances β1 integrin expression and mechanotransduction, leading to improved cardiac performance. Notably, increased β1D integrin has been shown to preserve sarcomere structure and enhance contractility under stress; mice lacking β1D develop dilated cardiomyopathy and reduced force generation [65, 66]. Furthermore, biomechanical studies indicate that increased adhesion force, through integrin clustering and ECM engagement, promotes cardiomyocyte alignment and force generation — key contributors to improved systolic wall thickening and myocardial blood flow during exercise. These findings align with our previous work showing that exercise training enhances systolic wall thickening and transmural myocardial perfusion, suggesting that the observed physiological improvements may be mediated by increased β1 integrin signaling and adhesion force. Thus, our current observations of increased myocardial stiffness and adhesion forces provide mechanistic insight into how integrin-dependent remodeling may support improved force generation in the setting of physiological stress, such as exercise.

Compared to collagen subunits, the role of fibronectin in adult myocardium is relatively not well understood. Fibronectin expression is stimulated after tissue injury in the heart [67]. Furthermore, fibronectin has been associated with fibrosis and impaired cardiac repair and regeneration [68]. Fibronectin induces cardiac progenitor cell expansion, resulting in regeneration and repair after myocardial infarction by interacting with β1 integrin-focal adhesion kinase mediation [18]. We also found that fibronectin interacting with β1 integrin-focal adhesion complex enhances myocyte contractility. However, cardiac progenitor cells are irrelevant to cardiac regeneration [69, 70], and further studies are warranted in the role of fibronectin in cardiac myocytes. Our laboratory has identified the direct role of fibronectin in cardiac muscle contractility modulation [19, 20]. Fibronectin binds to the integrin receptor via fibronectin region III, which contains RGD sequence repeats and assembles the focal adhesion complex, resulting in stimulation of various signaling pathways [20]. Fibronectin enhances myocyte force production and increases intracellular Ca2+, which activates L-type Ca2+ channel and contractile myofilament activation process [19]. While some studies report that fibronectin contributes to cardiac fibrosis, it also plays a critical role in the early phase of cardiac repair, particularly through interactions with β1 integrin and activation of FAK–PI3K/Akt or ERK/MAPK signaling pathways [18, 71]. In adult infarcted hearts, exogenous fibronectin delivery has been shown to enhance the retention and survival of transplanted cardiac progenitor cells (CPCs) and functions as a biological scaffold to support regeneration [18, 72]. To our knowledge, we are the first to report with the current study that exercise training increases fibronectin protein in ischemic myocardium resulting in increased force production.

When a coronary artery is occluded, oxygen supply to the downstream myocardium is significantly reduced. A prolonged ischemic condition (> 60 min) causes necrosis in cardiac myocytes, which stimulates the toll-like receptor pathway [17, 73, 74]. Chemokines and cytokines are then activated by NF-κB signaling, and immune cells infiltrate the area. Hence, infiltrated immune cells and activated chemokines and cytokines activate inflammatory signaling that further stimulate cytokines, chemokines, and growth factors. Cytokines, chemokines, growth factors, and inflammatory signaling induces cardiac remodeling by releasing remodeling enzymes [15, 73–75]. Growing evidence demonstrate the important roles of immune cells in cardiac remodeling, specifically as it relates to how macrophages are involved in the fibrotic process and how targeted therapy against IL6, IL1β and MCP1 could be beneficial in the infarction-induced heart failure in animal models [39–44]. Additionally, studies have demonstrated positive impact of acute and chronic exercise on the immune cells and cardiovascular health and how exercise-training prevents disease by lessening immunosenescence in the older population [76–80]. It has been shown that exercise-training positively impact the ECM protein changes and matrix metalloproteinase and their tissue inhibitors and improve cardiac function in a diabetic cardiomyopathy mouse model [76]. IL1β contributes to cardiac fibrosis through STAT3-IL11 signaling, suggesting IL6 could be a potential therapeutic target for cardiac fibrosis [42]. Studies have reported that anti-MCP1 gene therapy prevented post-myocardial infarction heart failure in a mouse model and blockade of IL1β improved cardiac remodeling after acute myocardial infarction [41, 43]. CD11b+ cells mediate recruitments of various leukocytes, including monocytes, neutrophils, natural killer cells, granulocytes, and macrophages. Pharmacological blockade or gene knockout of CD11b significantly attenuated cardiac remodeling and macrophage infiltration and polarization in the hypertensive mouse models [44]. Our data demonstrate that collateral-dependent myocardial regions of sedentary pigs showed CD11b+ cell infiltration while exercise training ameliorated the immune cells (Figure 6). Exercise-training decreased pro-inflammatory signaling genes such as IL6, IL1β, and MCP1 (Figure 6) suggesting that immune cell infiltration and pro-inflammatory signaling may mediate fibrotic remodeling. Taken together, our data corroborate well with the notion that exercise-training impacts positively on cardiac remodeling and regulates the inflammatory signal in balance to improve cardiac function.

In summary, novel findings from the current study demonstrate that exercise training promotes fibronectin expression in the collateral-dependent region of the ischemic porcine heart. Exercise training also protects against coronary artery occlusion-induced fibrosis and increases myocyte force production that provides a cardioprotective effect in coronary artery disease. Increased myocardial force generation after exercise training may be in part attributable to enhanced β integrin-mediated focal adhesion complex assembly. Increased immune cell infiltration and pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines result in fibrosis in ischemic myocardium while exercise training blunts immune cell infiltration and associated inflammatory signaling that may result in distinct ECM remodeling (Figure 7).

caption.title: Coronary artery occlusion induces fibrotic remodeling in the left ventricle while exercise training ameliorates stenosis-induced fibrosis in the swine left ventricle.
caption.p: A) Masson’s trichrome staining was carried out on nonoccluded (nonocc) and collateral-dependent (coll-dep) left ventricular myocardial sections from sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex) trained swine. Blue color indicates fibrotic areas, and red indicates a normal cellular area. Images were obtained with x10 objective (NA = 0.5). B) The fibrotic area percentage was calculated and plotted (n = 8 field of view from three animals/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p < 0.05 vs. respective nonoccluded. # indicates p < 0.05 vs. sedentary collateral-dependent.

caption.title: Coronary artery occlusion induces fibrotic remodeling in the left ventricle by increasing collagens while exercise training ameliorates ameroid-occluded fibrosis in swine left ventricles.
caption.p: A). Representative images of collagen I and collagen III in nonoccluded (nonocc) and collateral-dependent (coll-dep) left ventricular myocardial sections from sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex) trained pigs. Images were obtained with x10 (upper panel, NA = 0.5) and x63 (lower panel, NA = 1.4) objectives. B) Quantification of fluorescence signal intensity of collagen I and collagen III are plotted (n = 15 field of view from three animals/group). * indicates p < 0.05 vs. nonoccluded. # indicates p < 0.05 vs. sedentary. C) Representative fibronectin images in nonoccluded and collateral-dependent left ventricular myocardial sections of sedentary and exercise training animals. Images were obtained with x10 (upper panel, NA = 0.5) and x63 (lower panel, NA = 1.4) and objectives. D) Quantification of fibronectin-positive fluorescence signal intensity (n=15 field of view from three animals/group) was plotted. E) Representative images of laminin in nonoccluded and collateral-dependent left ventricular myocardial sections of sedentary and exercise trained pigs. Images were obtained with x10 (upper panel, NA = 0.5) and x40 (lower panel, NA = 1.42) objectives. F) Quantification of laminin-positive fluorescence signal intensity (n = 15 field of view from three animals/group) was plotted. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p < 0.05 vs. respective nonoccluded. # indicates p < 0.05 vs. respective sedentary.

caption.title: Exercise-training increased β1 integrin but no changes in α5 and αv integrins while β3 integrin expression is high in sedentary myocardium.
caption.p: A) Representative Western blots show β1 integrin, β3 integrin, and GAPDH in nonoccluded (nonocc) and collateral-dependent (coll-dep) left ventricular myocardial sections of sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex)-trained pigs. B) The relative expression of β1 integrin/GAPDH and C) β3 integrin/GAPDH (n=4) were quantified and plotted. D) Representative Western blots show α5 integrin, αv integrin, and GAPDH in nonoccluded (nonocc) and collateral-dependent (coll-dep) left ventricular myocardial sections of sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex)-trained pigs. E) The relative expression of α5 integrin/GAPDH and F) αv integrin/GAPDH were quantified and plotted (n = 3/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p < 0.05 vs. respective sedentary. # indicates p < 0.05 vs. respective nonoccluded.

caption.title: Exercise training enhances adhesion force and membrane stiffness.
caption.p: A) Analyses of force-density plots of adhesion events during fibronectin-coated probe retraction in cardiomyocytes from sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex) trained swine. The observed adhesion force and the corresponding number of events in the experiments (50 curves/cell for a total of 500 curves) were plotted as histograms. Solid lines represent the results that fitted with multiple Gaussian distributions. Insets: Fibronectin binding probabilities (solid bars). B) The adhesion force representing the first peak force was calculated from cardiomyocytes of sedentary and exercise-trained pigs in the absence or presence of α5-integrin, αv-integrin or α11-integrin (served as control) mAb. The adhesion force was significantly increased by exercise training (500 total curves from 10 myocytes per animal and three animals/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. **** indicates p < 0.0001 vs. sedentary control. C) The cell stiffness, which provides a metric reflecting the contractility, was increased by exercise training (500 total curves from 10 myocytes per animal from three animals/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. *** indicates p < 0.001.

caption.title: Trabeculae fibers from exercise-trained pigs shows enhanced force generation that is mediated by focal adhesion kinase.
caption.p: The myocardial force generation was assessed in trabeculae fibers from the left ventricle of sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex)-trained pigs with or without soluble fibronectin or focal adhesion kinase inhibitor (n=7 animals/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p<0.05 vs. control (Con). # indicates p<0.05 vs. fibronectin. ^ indicates p<0.05 compared to sedentary control.

caption.title: Coronary artery occlusion induces inflammatory signaling while exercise training decrease inflammation in the collateral-dependent region.
caption.p: A) Immunofluorescence images of CD11b+ immune cells, nuclei (DAPI), and merged images in nonoccluded (nonocc) and collateral-dependent (coll-dep) left ventricular myocardium from sedentary (Sed) and exercise (Ex)-trained animals using x40 objective (NA = 1.3). CD11b+ cells were counted and normalized by total nuclei and plotted (n = 9 field of view from three animals/group). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p < 0.05 vs. nonoccluded. # indicates p<0.05 vs. sedentary coll-dep. B and C) Induction of inflammatory cytokine mRNA in coronary artery occlusion of myocardium from sedentary and exercise-trained swine. Expression of IL-6, IL-1β, and MCP-1 was analyzed. Real-time quantification was performed and fold change relative to the sedentary group. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. * indicates p < 0.05 vs. sedentary.

caption.title: Schematic representation of exercise training-mediated coronary artery disease-induced fibrotic remodeling and cardiac contractility.
caption.p: Coronary artery disease increases collagen I and collagen III protein levels. Immune cells are infiltrated, and pro-inflammatory mediators are increased in the collateral-dependent myocardium of chronically occluded swine. The β3 integrin expression is increased by coronary artery occlusion that contributes to decreased cardiac contractility. On the other hand, exercise training ameliorates cardiac fibrosis by decreasing collagen I and collagen III protein levels. Fibronectin protein content is enhanced by exercise training as well as β1 integrin that promotes contractility.
Efficacy of the exercise training program

fn.p: Values are mean ± S.E.M. Numbers in parentheses represent number of animals; CS, citrate synthase; BW, body weight; HW/BW, heart-to-body weight ratio; TMH, triceps medial head;
